next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The rain had quit before dawn, but the hillside kept moving after it, giving little sighs underfoot and settling the whole graveyard deeper into the mountain. By the time Della came up the path from Cedar Bench, the lower end of Old Sion was already ringed with people who had no work there. They stood under cloud-torn light with their collars up and their Sunday curiosity on their faces, waiting to see whether the exposed coffin in the fever plot belonged to a Bledsoe, a Wolfe, or just another patch of old trouble the valley could worry to death before noon.

The dam sounded once downriver, a low concussion that came through the wet air more than through the ear. It shook water from the cedar branches and sent a thin rill of clay sliding across the path. Della put one hand against the fencepost until the tremor finished moving through the ground. Her left thumb had swollen stiff in the night. It would not bend cleanly around the shovel handle now. Her lower back felt packed with broken crockery. She had slept three hours at most, and none of them deeply enough to make her forget what the rain had done.

The landslip had bitten farther into the bank. Yesterday only the coffin edge had shown. This morning half the box lay naked to the air, pitched crooked from the hill with one end buried and the other hanging out over the wash in a half-risen lurch. The lid was sprung along one seam. A cedar root as thick as Della's wrist had shouldered through the clay just above it and left the whole slope looking pried open by something patient and stronger than any of them....
open_hooks_to_carry: The dam schedule is tightened and explained in practical terms.
Della's physical strain is rendered concretely through hands, back, and breath.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...if weighing whether gratitude was worth the trouble of speaking. In the end she only said, "He wrote me out while I was still alive enough to feel it." Then she carried her beans back into the hall and shut the door softly behind her.

Lucille waited until the latch caught. "You understand what this does."

"I do now."

"It strengthens Gabriel. It strengthens Noreen. It also gives Vernon reason to fight ugly and fast. If you go forward, there won't be a narrow version anymore."

Della looked at the envelope with the watch inside it. She could still feel the blunt round of the thing in her palm, the dead weight of time carried uselessly all these years. "There never was," she said. "I just kept pretending there was a grave-sized truth and the rest could stay buried around it."

Lucille's face eased, not into comfort, but into the smaller expression that meant respect had beaten impatience for once. "All right," she said. "Then we stop pretending."

Outside, another dam blast rolled under the schoolhouse floor, distant but strong enough to shake chalk dust loose from the windowsill. Lucille laid one hand over the envelope and the map as if steadying them against the vibration. Della watched that hand and understood, with a clarity more punishing than panic, that naming Gabriel would not simply call one dead man back into the record. It would drag daylight across the way this valley had been portioned, softened, and stolen in decent language for longer than she had let herself admit. There was no path left now that led only to the fever plot. The orchard had made sure of that.
state_deltas_required: Keep the cemetery work moving through rain, mud, and an accelerated state timetable.
The fever plot opens further in a landslip, making the identification crisis immediate and unavoidable.
